data.title: FEDERAL HOME LOAN BANK OF CINCINNATI ANNOUNCES RESULTS OF DIRECTOR ELECTIONS
data.description:
Berry, Barnes, Herron elected to four-year terms; Pell to serve as board chair; Diaz as vice chair

CINCINNATI, Nov. 21, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — The Federal Home Loan Bank of Cincinnati's Board of Directors announced the results of the FHLB's 2025 director elections and board leadership updates. In this election, two Kentucky Member Directors and one at-large Independent Director were elected to the Board. From Kentucky, J. Wade Berry and Timothy E. Barnes were elected as Member Directors. Danny J. Herron, Nashville, was elected to serve as an Independent Public Interest Director. All three directors will serve four-year terms commencing January 1, 2026, and expiring December 31, 2029. Michael P. Pell will serve as chair filling a one-year vacancy effective January 1, 2026 through December 31, 2026, and Lewis Diaz will serve as vice chair for a two-year term commencing January 1, 2026 through December 31, 2027.

J. Wade Berry is President & CEO of Farmers Bank & Trust Company, Marion, Kentucky. He has served on the FHLB Board as a Member Director since 2022, and currently chairs its Risk Committee.

Timothy E. Barnes is President & CEO, Hometown Bank, Corbin, Kentucky. He was newly elected to the Board.

Danny J. Herron is President & CEO of Habitat for Humanity of Greater Nashville, Tennessee. He has served on the FHLB Board since 2022, as a Public Interest Independent Director, and he currently chairs the Board's Governance Committee.

Michael P. Pell is President and CEO, First State Bank, Winchester, Ohio. He has served on the FHLB Board as an Ohio Member Director since 2019, and as Board Vice Chair from January 1, 2025-December 31, 2025.

Lewis Diaz is a Partner at Dinsmore and Sholl, LLP, Covington, Kentucky. He has served on the FHLB Board since 2022, as a Public Interest Independent Director, and he currently chairs the Board's Human Resources, Compensation and Inclusion Committee.

About the FHLB

The FHLB is a AA+ rated wholesale cooperative bank owned by 601 member financial institutions, including commercial banks, thrifts, credit unions, insurance companies and community development financial institutions in Kentucky, Ohio and Tennessee. The FHLB provides members access to products and services (primarily Advances, which are a readily available, low-cost source of funds, purchases of certain mortgage loans from members, and issuance of Letters of Credit to members) and a competitive return through quarterly dividends on their capital investment in the FHLB. The FHLB funds these products and services by raising private-sector capital from member-stockholders and, with the other Federal Home Loan Banks (FHLBanks) in the FHLBank System, issuing high-quality debt in the global capital markets. The FHLB also funds community investment programs that help its members create affordable housing and promote community economic development.

This news release may contain forward-looking statements that are subject to risks and uncertainties including, but not limited to, the effects of economic market conditions on demand for the FHLB's products, legislative or regulatory developments concerning the FHLB System, competitive forces and other risks detailed from time to time in the FHLB's filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The forward-looking statements speak as of the date made and are not guarantees of future performance. Actual results or developments may differ materially from the expectations expressed or implied in the forward-looking statements, and the FHLB undertakes no obligation to update any such statements.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/federal-home-loan-bank-of-cincinnati-announces-results-of-director-elections-302623528.html

SOURCE Federal Home Loan Bank of Cincinnati

data.title: CATAPULT BOOKS TO ACQUIRE HAWTHORNE BOOKS & LITERARY ARTS
data.description:
NEW YORK, Nov. 21, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Catapult Book Group has acquired Portland Oregon-based independent press Hawthorne Books & Literary Arts, which will join Catapult, Counterpoint Press, and Soft Skull Press as the fourth imprint of the group. Under the deal, Catapult, an award-winning literary publishing house founded by Elizabeth R. Koch and operating within Unlikely Collaborators Inc, has purchased Hawthorne's catalog of titles, as well as the Hawthorne trademark.

Additionally, distribution for Hawthorne Books will move from Publishers Group West to Catapult's distributor Penguin Random House. Hawthorne Books will continue to publish new releases with its first acquisition scheduled for Fall 2026.

"We were immediately drawn to how closely Hawthorne's sensibility mirrors our own," said Catapult Publisher Alyson Forbes. "Hawthorne has long published writers who probe beneath the surface of experience, work that echoes Catapult's mission to illuminate complexity and broaden perspective. Bringing our lists together strengthens our shared commitment to stories that resonate on both an emotional and reflective level and ensures readers have more opportunities to discover writing that is insightful and meaningful."

Hawthorne Books was founded in 2001 by Publisher and Executive Editor Rhonda Hughes. With the acquisition, Catapult gains a well-established backlist of critically acclaimed titles such as Lidia Yuknavitch's The Chronology of Water (now a major motion picture directed by Kristen Stewart), Poe Ballantine's Love and Terror on the Howling Plains of Nowhere, Frank Meeink and Jody M. Roy's Autobiography of a Recovering Skinhead, and most recently Sidney Morrison's novel Frederick Douglass, among many others.

Hughes will stay on with Catapult, serving as Contributing Editor for Hawthorne Books, acquiring on behalf of the imprint, and reporting to group Editorial Director Dan Smetanka.

In a statement, Hughes remarked, "Having Hawthorne become an imprint alongside such esteemed publishers as Catapult, Counterpoint Press, and Soft Skull Press--and having the opportunity to work with the remarkable Catapult team--is both a joy and an honor for me and for our authors. This new chapter has reaffirmed my deep appreciation for the art and spirit of publishing."

About Catapult

Catapult is an independent publishing collective created by the founders of Electric Literature and Black Balloon Publishing, comprising the imprints Catapult, Counterpoint Press, Soft Skull Press, and Hawthorne Books. Operating within Unlikely Collaborators, the nonprofit founded by Elizabeth R. Koch, Catapult's authors have received major recognition, including the International Booker Prize, Andrew Carnegie Medal for Excellence in Fiction, National Book Critics Circle Award, and was a finalist for several National Book Awards.

Anchored in the Perception Box(TM) framework developed by Koch, Catapult champions a writing process grounded in self-reflection and curiosity. We support authors who examine the beliefs and experiences that shape their perspectives, creating work that challenges assumptions, reveals nuance, and invites readers to expand the way they see themselves and one another.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/catapult-books-to-acquire-hawthorne-books--literary-arts-302623580.html

SOURCE Catapult Book Group

data.title: Lululemon's Recent Leadership Changes Seen as Negative — Market Talk
data.description: Lululemon's leadership rejiggering could pose further challenges for the athleticwear brand, Jefferies analysts say in a note. Celeste Burgoyne, who oversaw all customer-facing aspects of Lululemon's North American business, is departing the retailer and will be succeeded by Andre Maestrini, who has been leading Lululemon's international business and has been appointed to a consolidated role of president and chief commercial officer, the analysts say. It's unclear if Maestrini is the right choice to navigate Lululemon's recent challenges in North America, including a decline in demand, given his experience is heavily weighted toward international markets, the analysts say. Maestrini is also based in London, and it's unclear if he will relocate to join the rest of the executive team, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Corn bids steady-higher, soy steady
data.description:
Basis bids for corn were steady to higher while soy bids were steady in the U.S. Midwest on Friday during relatively quiet trade, dealers said.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture this week confirmed that China bought more than 1.5 million metric tons of American soybeans. Traders said they had largely expected deals of that size before the USDA reported them, and they had factored China's buying into the market already.

  • Most-active soybean futures rose 2-1/2 cents to $11.25 per bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade.

  • CBOT March corn (CH26) fell 1/4 cent to end at $4.37-1/2 per bushel

data.title: Nordea Bank Abp: Repurchase of own shares on 21.11.2025
data.description:
Nordea

Nordea Bank Abp

Stock exchange release – Changes in company’s own shares

21.11.2025 at 22.30 EET

Nordea Bank Abp (LEI: 529900ODI3047E2LIV03) has on 21.11.2025 completed repurchases of own shares (ISIN: FI4000297767) as follows:

Trading venue (MIC Code)

* FX rate used: SEK to EUR 11.0212 and DKK to EUR 7.4686

** Rounded to two decimals

On 16 October 2025, Nordea announced a share buy-back programme of up to a maximum of EUR 250 million based on the authorisation granted by Nordea’s Annual General Meeting 2025. The repurchase of own shares is executed in public trading in accordance with Regulation No. 596/2014 of the European Parliament and Council of 16 April 2014 (MAR) and the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2016/1052.

After the disclosed transactions, Nordea holds 2,566,080 treasury shares for capital optimisation purposes and 10,299,096 treasury shares for remuneration purposes.

Details of each transaction are included as an appendix to this announcement.

On behalf of Nordea Bank Abp,

Morgan Stanley Europe SE

For further information:

Ilkka Ottoila, Head of Investor Relations, +358 9 5300 7058

Media inquiries, +358 10 416 8023 or press@nordea.com

data.title: Front Month ICE Brent Crude Fell 2.84% This Week to Settle at $62.56 — Data Talk
data.description:
Front Month ICE Brent Crude for Jan. delivery lost $1.83 per barrel, or 2.84% to $62.56 this week

  • Largest one week net and percentage decline since the week ending Oct. 3, 2025
  • Down six of the past eight weeks
  • Today it is down 82.00 cents or 1.29%
  • Down for three consecutive sessions
  • Down $2.33 or 3.59% over the last three sessions
  • Largest three day dollar and percentage decline since Tuesday, Oct. 14, 2025
  • Down four of the past five sessions
  • Lowest settlement value since Tuesday, Oct. 21, 2025
  • Off 23.74% from its 52-week high of $82.03 hit Wednesday, Jan. 15, 2025
  • Up 3.87% from its 52-week low of $60.23 hit Monday, May 5, 2025
  • Down 16.78% from 52 weeks ago
  • Off 23.74% from its 2025 settlement high of $82.03 hit Wednesday, Jan. 15, 2025
  • Up 3.87% from its 2025 settlement low of $60.23 hit Monday, May 5, 2025
  • Off 57.17% from its record high of $146.08 hit Thursday, July 3, 2008
  • Month-to-date it is down 3.86%
  • Year-to-date it is down $12.08 or 16.18%

All prices are calculated based on the settlement price of the current front month contract.

Source: Dow Jones Market Data

data.title: PLATTS: 100--US FERC report on enforcement actions shows settlements, refunds in FY2025
data.description: (Platts)--21Nov2025/323 pm EST/2023 GMT **24 new investigations opened, 17 closed in FY2025 **Pending cases include demand response penalties The US Federal Energy Regulatory Commission reported on market oversight actions in fiscal year 2025 that resulted in settlements with parties totaling $36.57 million. Several cases pending in courts or at the regulatory level are not included in that total. The number of settlements approved in FY2025 — 13 — is close to the 12 settlements approved in FY2024, and the report, released Nov. 20, shows similar data trends in many respects, FERC staff told commissioners during their Nov. 20 open meeting. The number of investigation cases opened and closed in the last two fiscal years is similar, as is the number of self-reports by companies on activities discovered that may trigger FERC enforcement action, FERC staff told Commissioner David LaCerte in response to questions. The money obtained through settlements includes $22.84 million in civil penalties and $13.73 million in disgorgement by the companies involved, according to the report (AD07-13). Those totals do not include a $5 million payment to resolve an administrative proceeding or a $30,000 penalty by a hydropower project owner, FERC said in the report. In August, FERC's Office of Enforcement was renamed the Office of Enforcement and Regulatory Accounting. The office is made up of three divisions, with one covering investigations, another on audits and accounting, and a third on analytics and surveillance. During FY2025, which ran from Oct 1, 2024, to Sept. 30, 2025, the Division of Audits and Accounting completed 10 audits of public utility, natural gas, and oil pipeline companies. The audits resulted in $80 million in refunds and other recoveries, 63 findings of noncompliance and 260 recommendations for corrective action, FERC said in the report. Among the cases that are pending in either court litigation or regulatory proceedings are proposed penalties of $722 million against American Efficient (IN24-2); $60 million against Rover Pipeline and Energy Transfer Partners (IN17-4, IN19-4); $25 million against Ketchup Caddy (IN23-14); and $15 million against Boyce Hydro Power (P-10809). FERC enforcement staff opened 24 new investigations in FY2025, while bringing 17 investigations to a close without further action, said Kevin Dinan, the staffer in the enforcement office who presented the report. "I sat in your seat," FERC Chairman Laura Swett told Dinan, when, about 10 years ago, she was in the enforcement office and presented the annual report to commissioners. "I know from personal experience that the industry waits with baited breath for this report every year," Swett said, because it shows FERC market oversight trends and enforcement activities. In the report, FERC included examples of referrals from regional grid market monitors that are reviewed and closed without an investigation. The companies or individuals are not identified, but "these summaries can be helpful to companies seeking to comply with the commission's regulations and orders," FERC said. When Swett asked Dinan about any trends seen in the current report, he said one constant in many of the cases is the need for companies to meet their obligations imposed by the terms of service they are providing. A power generator that signs up as a capacity resource, for example, needs to provide power at the level of timeliness and reliability upon which payment is made to avoid a violation, Dinan said. Similarly, for a company providing demand response services in an organized wholesale market, "it must have actual customers that agree to provide a response consistent, again, with the terms of the demand response program it agreed to participate in," Dinan told Swett. A good compliance department in any entity will be familiar with such commitments and work with its staff to ensure commitments are met, he said. That may have been a reference to a couple of the pending demand response penalty cases, in which Ketchup Caddy and American Efficient have been alleged to have been paid for demand response commitments that were not met, and with limited information on customers. SUPREME COURT CASE IMPLICATIONS In the American Efficient case, the company recently asked FERC to decline to issue the proposed penalty, repeating its claim that FERC's enforcement order violated Supreme Court precedent in SEC v. Jarkesy. In the FERC staff investigation and show cause order proposing a penalty, staff alleged that American Efficient bought sales data of energy-efficient products at retail outlets, calculated how much power would be saved with those products and bid the energy savings into capacity markets as if it caused the savings. FERC staff alleged that the company received millions of dollars in capacity payments from PJM Interconnection and the Midcontinent ISO without providing the promised benefits. The report states that aspects of FERC enforcement processes have been challenged in light of the Supreme Court's 2024 decision in SEC v. Jarkesy. In that case, the court said the US Securities and Exchange Commission's use of administrative law judges to adjudicate civil penalty actions in securities fraud cases violated the right to a jury trial under the Seventh Amendment of the Constitution. The report noted that the enforcement office's Division of Analytics and Surveillance, which examines energy trading and other activities, made improvements in its surveillance processes during FY2025 by using more sophisticated, interactive dashboards and moving analytic capabilities to cloud computing. The migration to cloud computing allowed access to nonpublic and commercial data feeds, enabling FERC staff to compile analyses that previously required ad-hoc activities, FERC said. — Thomas Tiernan, thomas.tiernan@spglobal.com-- Edited by Michael Lustig, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Wheat Futures Higher on Quiet Pre-Holiday Trading - Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for March delivery rose 0.3%, to $5.42 1/2 a bushel, on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, with trading seen as low volume ahead of next week's Thanksgiving holiday.
  • Soybeans for January delivery rose 0.2%, to $11.26 3/4 a bushel.
  • Corn for December delivery fell 0.1%, to $4.37 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Out of Office: Traders appeared to go into holiday mode, with wheat and the row crop complex in general showing only small moves. With the Thanksgiving holiday next week, traders seem to favor a risk-off approach. The holiday also marks the beginning of traders getting their books in order for the new year, which often involves liquidating long positions, said Charlie Sernatinger of Marex in a note, adding that if something moves the market next week then wheat may break out of its typical seasonal selling.

No Notice: The USDA didn't announce any fresh flash sales Friday of soybean exports to China, which concerned grain traders. "Stale length exits as China struggles with an abundance of domestic and world soybeans," said AgResource in a note. The most-active soybean contract is unchanged as analysts speculate how much China has purchased, and how much more it may buy. "China may add to its purchases on deeper corrections, but China has no willingness to chase a CBOT rally," said AgResource.

INSIGHT

Growth Potential: Demand for meat is likely to support growth in cattle and hog markets heading into the new year, said Teucrium Trading in a note. "Global income growth supports long-run protein consumption, sustaining baseline demand for feed grains," said Teucrium. Growing livestock supplies mean that more animal feed is needed, which favors soybean and corn futures.

Switching Things Up: Grain traders are concerned about demand for wheat globally, which in turn is pressuring prices in the short-term. But that is expected to turn the corner as 2026 progresses. "This is because the low price level--wheat prices on CBOT and Euronext fell to five-year lows in October--may have led to a reduction in the area sown with winter wheat," said Commerzbank in a note. The decrease in winter wheat planted around the world may make the supply situation tighter and raise prices. The firm moves its outlook for average 2026 wheat prices to $5.80 a bushel.

AHEAD

  • The USDA will release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Monday.
  • The USDA and the CBOT will be closed in observance of Thanksgiving on Thursday.
  • The CBOT will be open for a half-day of trading, closing at 1:15 p.m. ET on Friday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Kinetik Holdings Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
ICE Review: Weakness in Canola Ahead of Weekend
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Intercontinental Exchange canola futures fell back on Friday, as the recent rally turns into rangebound trading.

An analyst said that without any fresh news canola has "no appetite to go higher."

Today's losses saw the January canola contract slip behind its 20-day moving average, but it remained above the 50-day average.

Added to that, weakness in Chicago soyoil, MATIF rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil put pressure on canola. Increases in Chicago soybeans and soymeal tried to temper the declines. Losses in crude oil weighed on vegetable oil values.

There was some good news for canola, as the Canadian Grain Commission reported the oilseed's exports for the week ended Nov. 16 improved more than 57% at a marketing-year high of 284,600 tons. However, the year-to-date for 2025/26 of 1.83 million tons is slightly more than the year ago exports.

The Canadian dollar was a pinch lower on Friday afternoon with the loonie at 70.95 U.S. cents compared to Thursday's close of 71.02.

There were 38,768 contracts traded on Friday, compared to 39,056 on Thursday. Spreading accounted for 24,562 contracts traded.

 
Prices are in Canadian dollars per metric ton: 
 
Canola  Price      Change 
Jan     641.10    dn  9.10 
Mar     653.70    dn  9.30 
May     664.40    dn  8.60 
Jul     670.70    dn  7.40

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.title: PLATTS: 100--Alberta TIER carbon prices rally on federal budget support
data.description: (Platts)--21Nov2025/325 pm EST/2025 GMT **Prices rise 11.8% on week to reach C$19/mtCO2e Nov. 20 **Speculative demand, year-end positioning support prices Alberta's Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction carbon prices have rebounded following the passage of Canada's federal budget on Nov. 17, which seeks to improve the effectiveness of Canada's carbon markets. Sources stated Nov. 20 that the federal budget's provisions for carbon markets serve as a key driver of renewed interest, providing greater policy certainty for emissions trading and strengthening Canada's carbon pricing framework. Platts assessed Alberta TIER Emission Performance Credits and Emission Offsets at C$19.00/metric ton of carbon dioxide equivalent on Nov. 20, up C$2/mtCO2e on the week. Platts is part of S&P Global Energy. Traded levels rose throughout the week, with a trade for EPCs and Offsets at C$19/mtCO2e on Nov. 20, the highest reported trade since the launch of the assessment on Aug. 1, 2025. "Sounds like the market expects new policy to support TIER and Canadian project development," one broker said. The broker previously noted that concerns were raised about the proposed elimination of the emissions cap on oil and gas in Canada, highlighting significant pushback unless there is strong support from markets like TIER. Canada is experiencing a political movement aimed at increasing exports, particularly of LNG and crude oil, the broker said, but added that environmental groups are advocating for higher carbon taxes and stricter emissions allowances to support this growth. Additionally, market participants have observed that the TIER market has reached the floor, prompting a turnaround in prices. A second broker explained that there was a lack of sellers when bid levels ranged from C$16.50/mtCO2e to C$17.60/mtCO2e, which required rising bid levels to attract supply. The second broker also noted a slight uptick in demand as the year-end approaches, with entities working to balance their emissions for 2025. Adding to the upward support, a trader heard that a US speculative entity has entered the Alberta carbon market, seeking profit opportunities that could further support prices. "I've heard that there are some new spec in the US looking to buy some credits, [which] should support prices a bit," the trader said. TIER prices have experienced an extended period of weakness due to persistent oversupply and downturn post proposed program updates on Sept. 16. However, market participants continue to express bullish sentiment while offering minimal feedback on where prices may settle. — Madeline Ryan, madeline.ryan@spglobal.com; David Lademan, david.lademan@spglobal.com-- Edited by Karina Roman, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Jacobs Solutions Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
Franklin Street Properties in Talks to Refinance Debt
data.description:
By Terell Wright

Franklin Street Properties is in active negotiations with a lender to refinance all of its current debt, commercial real-estate group said.

The company in May said its board of directors had initiated a review of ways to maximize shareholder value, including a potential sale or debt refinancing.

Shares rose 9.8%, to 98 cents, late Friday, though remain down 46% this year.

FSP Chief Executive George Carter said the company doesn't know of any specific reason for the recent decline in its shares.

Write to Terell Wright at terell.wright@wsj.com
